unit.name: Evaluation bottlenecks and future directions
unit.description: Summarizes the hardest open problems Li emphasizes at the end of the lecture: evaluation, simulation realism, benchmark design, and the move from foundation policies toward foundation world models.
unit.summary: The final segment argues that evaluation is one of the field’s biggest unresolved bottlenecks. Real-world evaluation is still the default, but it is expensive, noisy, slow, and sensitive to factors like initial conditions, lighting, and friction [ts=4439s] [ts=4457s] [ts=4471s] [ts=4484s]. Li stresses that policy training loss often measures only one-step prediction quality and correlates weakly with long-horizon real-world success, which makes model selection difficult [ts=4489s] [ts=4505s] [ts=4516s] [ts=4528s]. Simulation-based evaluation helps but still faces sim-to-real gaps, difficult asset creation, world digitalization, and the need for realistic procedural generation [ts=4546s] [ts=4569s] [ts=4579s] [ts=4593s]. He says the community wants an ImageNet-like embodied benchmark where progress on the benchmark really means progress in robot learning [ts=4602s] [ts=4618s]. He closes by pointing to foundation world models trained from large action-conditioned robot interaction datasets as an important future direction beyond foundation policies alone [ts=4630s] [ts=4649s] [ts=4663s].

key_points.text: Real-world evaluation is costly, noisy, and highly sensitive to setup details such as lighting, friction, and initial configuration.
Low training loss on one-step predictions can fail to predict long-horizon task success.
Simulation evaluation still struggles with sim-to-real gap, asset generation, digitalization, and realistic procedural scene generation.
Embodied AI still lacks an ImageNet-like benchmark whose gains reliably transfer to real robot progress.
A promising direction is to train foundation world models from large action-conditioned robot interaction datasets.

kp_catalog.name: Evaluation bottlenecks and foundation world models
kp_catalog.description: Robot learning progress is hard to measure because real-world evaluation is expensive and simulation is imperfect, motivating better benchmarks and foundation world models trained from action-conditioned data.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because evaluation bottlenecks and foundation world models clarifies robot learning progress is hard to measure because real-world evaluation is expensive and simulation is imperfect, motivating better benchmarks and foundation world models trained from action-conditioned data, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Robotic foundation models with pretraining and post-training
kp_catalog.description: Robotic foundation models aim for broad goal-conditioned action generalization by combining large aggregated robot datasets with pretrained VLMs and task-specific post-training.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because robotic foundation models with pretraining and post-training formalizes how robotic foundation models aim for broad goal-conditioned action generalization by combining large aggregated robot datasets with pretrained VLMs and task-specific post-training, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Learned forward dynamics for model-based planning
kp_catalog.description: Instead of directly learning a policy, a robot can learn how actions change the world and use that model to optimize future action sequences.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because learned forward dynamics for model-based planning formalizes how instead of directly learning a policy, a robot can learn how actions change the world and use that model to optimize future action sequences, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
